text.title: Research at Scale Pipeline and Subagents
text.summary: Nine-stage pipeline, ten-subagent table with model assignments, and the frozen-codebook pattern for preventing theme drift.

list.item: 1. intake: convert PM brief to study spec, screener, hypotheses
2. sampling: build quotas, randomize, dedupe panel respondents
3. instrumentation: configure unmoderated test scripts in Maze/Userlytics
4. collection: execute tests and gather session recordings
5. transcription: forced-aligned transcription with diarization, PII redaction
6. coding: apply frozen codebook, tag quotes with codes and valence
7. synthesis: cross-segment pattern mining, confidence-tiered findings

rule.statement: Run theme-coder and bias-auditor in parallel; gate report-writer on a researcher human-in-loop checkpoint at step 8.
rule.rationale: AI synthesis can proceed while bias audit runs — they are independent. But publishing without a human checkpoint after synthesis lets AI-invented "insights" reach stakeholders without review.

rule.statement: Assign Haiku to mechanical tasks (panel-sampler, protocol-runner, transcript-cleaner, dashboard-publisher); Sonnet to structured synthesis (theme-coder, quote-curator, bias-auditor); Opus to cross-segment pattern mining and executive synthesis.
rule.rationale: This assignment minimizes cost while matching model capability to task complexity. Inverting it — Opus on transcript cleaning, Haiku on pattern mining — either burns tokens or degrades quality.

list.item: research-intake (Sonnet): PM brief → study-spec.md + screener JSON
panel-sampler (Haiku): quotas, randomize, dedupe → participant CSV
protocol-runner (Haiku): execute unmoderated tests in Maze/Userlytics → session IDs
transcript-cleaner (Haiku): strip filler, redact PII, normalize timestamps → clean VTT/JSON
theme-coder (Sonnet): apply codebook, tag quotes with codes + valence → coded JSONL
pattern-analyst (Opus): cross-segment clustering, confidence-tiered findings → findings draft
quote-curator (Sonnet): representative + outlier quotes per finding → quote bank
report-writer (Opus): executive synthesis, recommendations, confidence tiers → final report MD
bias-auditor (Sonnet): leading phrasing, sample bias detection → audit notes
dashboard-publisher (Haiku): push themes/sentiment to Looker/Notion/Dovetail → URLs

rule.statement: Lock the codebook before collection; version it (v1.0.0) and bump only with documented diffs; use a separate proposed_codes overflow channel instead of modifying the codebook mid-study.
rule.rationale: Theme drift across batches is the #1 AI coding failure mode. A frozen codebook with a version + proposed_codes channel surfaces emergent themes for human review without corrupting the existing taxonomy.

example.note: Theme-coder prompt with frozen codebook and proposed_codes channel:
example.code: <task>code-transcript</task> <codebook src=".aidocs/research/codebook.yaml"/> <rules> <rule>One quote may receive multiple codes; record span offsets.</rule> <rule>Reject codes not in codebook. Emit proposed_codes list instead.</rule> <rule>Tag valence in {-2,-1,0,1,2} with one-line evidence.</rule> <rule>If transcript is less than 80% English confidence, halt and request translation.</rule> </rules> <output schema="coded_quote_v1">JSONL</output> <gotcha>Do not summarize. Preserve verbatim spans.</gotcha>

rule.statement: Sample 10% of AI-coded segments for human re-coding every batch; track inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa >= 0.7) and recalibrate when it drops below threshold.
rule.rationale: AI coding accuracy drifts as transcripts vary in quality and subject matter. Kappa tracking catches drift before it contaminates an entire study's findings.

text.reference: Frozen taxonomy with codes, valence defaults, examples, and segment axes.
Batch-coding shell script with dedup and parquet output.
